items.title: Overview
items.text: This document explains which directories and files on the ARI robot are persistent across reboots, which should be backed up, and how to change non-persistent configurations.

items.title: Persistent Directories
items.text:
Dir location Description Make copy Justification /media/root-ro Main filesystem (read-only) N Unique data removed on reinstall /boot/efi Bootloader filesystem N Same for each install /etc/calibration PAL-specific calibrations and user settings Y Needed for robot operation, unique since delivery /home User home dir, includes .ros and calibration Y Apps use calibration data from here /var/log Log files N~ Usually not unique, too large to copy

Also back up /etc/fstab (contains boot mounting and settings).

items.text:

lsblk

items.title: Changing Non-Persistent Configurations
items.text:
To edit configuration files (e.g., PID settings):

  1. go into admin mode: 

    su

  2. Remount filesystem as read-write and enter it: 

    sudo rw
chroot /ro

  3. Edit the file or install pkg (using vi if nano is not available): 
    sudo vi /opt/pal/gallium/share/ari_controller_configuration/config/pids_v2.yaml
  4. when done exit the filesystem: 
    exit
  5. Remount as read-only: 
    sudo ro
  6. then exit admin mode: 
    exit

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
• If unable to remount /ro as read-only, ensure no processes are using it.
  • Always back up important configuration files before making changes.

items.text: Intents are used in ARI's HRI system to interpret and respond to user commands and behaviors. The system is based on research and white papers from PAL Robotics.

items.title: Isabella Robot Overview
items.text: Isabella Robot is an advanced mobile platform designed for social interaction, perception, and autonomous navigation.

items.title: Key Features
items.text:
• Mobile Base: Durable navigation and movement capabilities
  • Perception: Depth camera, LiDAR, and social perception systems
  • Voice Interaction: Respeaker microphone array for audio processing
  • Intelligent Core: LLM-based decision making and intent recognition
  • ROS Integration: Full Robot Operating System support for extensibility

items.title: Architecture Highlights
items.text:
• Modular software design using ROS nodes and services
  • Persistent filesystem for data and learning
  • Network communication via Docker containerization
  • Real-time transform (TF) system for coordinate management

items.title: Quick Start Guide
items.text: Get Isabella Robot up and running in minutes.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Ubuntu 20.04 or later
  • Python 3.8+
  • ROS Noetic or newer
  • Docker and Docker Compose
  • Git

items.title: 1. Clone the Repository
items.text:
git clone https://github.com/NM-TAFE/Isabella_Robot_Doc.git
cd Isabella_Robot_Doc

items.title: 2. Install Dependencies
items.text: pip install -r requirements.txt

items.title: 3. Build Documentation Locally
items.text:
mkdocs serve

Visit http://localhost:8000 to view the documentation.

items.title: 4. Build Static Site
items.text:
mkdocs build

The static site will be generated in the site/ directory.

items.title: Running Tests
items.text: Isabella Robot includes comprehensive test suites for validation and verification.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Ensure you have the following installed:

  • pytest
  • unittest (included with Python)
  • Docker (for integration tests)

items.title: Unit Tests
items.text:
Run all unit tests:

pytest tests/unit/

Run specific test file:

pytest tests/unit/test_localizer.py -v

items.title: Integration Tests
items.text:
Run integration tests (requires Docker):

pytest tests/integration/

items.title: Test Coverage
items.text:
Generate coverage report:

pytest --cov=src tests/

items.title: Test Structure
items.text:
tests/
├── unit/              # Unit tests for individual components
├── integration/       # Integration tests for multiple systems
└── fixtures/          # Test data and configurations

items.title: Continuous Integration
items.text: Tests are automatically run on every pull request via GitHub Actions.

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
If tests fail:

  1. Ensure all dependencies are installed: pip install -r requirements.txt
  2. Check Docker daemon is running: docker status
  3. Review test output for specific error messages
  4. Check the GitHub repository for known issues

items.title: Contributing Tests
items.text:
When contributing new features, please include:

  • Unit tests for new functions
  • Integration tests for new systems
  • Documentation of test procedures

items.title: Description
items.text: The ARI robot provides a web-based presentation screen for displaying information and interacting with users. This can be accessed via a browser on the local network.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Accessible at http://ari-20c:11011/webapps/pages/pal_touch_web_display/
  • Used for presentations, user interaction, and status display.

items.title: Screen not loading
items.text:
• Check network connection to ARI.
  • Ensure the web server is running on the robot.

items.title: Depth Camera
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation No Location. Torso Front (RGB-D RealSense D435i), Torso Back (Intel Realsense T265, stereo-fisheye) related artical Depth Camera - Computerphile

items.title: Description
items.text: The ARI robot uses two depth cameras: a RealSense D435i on the torso front and an Intel Realsense T265 (stereo-fisheye) on the torso back. These provide RGB-D and stereo depth data for navigation and perception.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• The D435i provides RGB-D data for front-facing perception.
  • The T265 provides stereo-fisheye data for rear perception and localization.
  • Both are standard components in the ARI platform.

items.title: Camera not detected
items.text:
• Check physical connections and USB ports.
  • Verify drivers are installed and ROS nodes are running.

items.title: Poor depth quality
items.text:
• Clean camera lenses.
  • Ensure adequate lighting and no obstructions.

items.text:
note: our unit did not come with docking stations and we do not currently have any

info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation No Location. See ARI documentation for physical location related artical aruco_ros GitHub

items.title: Description
items.text: The docking station is used for automatic charging and parking of the ARI robot. It uses visual markers (e.g., ArUco) for alignment and docking.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Provides a safe and reliable way for ARI to recharge autonomously.
  • Uses visual fiducials for precise alignment.
  • Integrated with ARI's navigation and power management systems.

items.title: Robot fails to dock
items.text:
• Check visual markers are clean and visible.
  • Ensure docking station is powered and unobstructed.
  • Verify ARI's navigation and docking nodes are running.

items.title: Lidar
items.text:
note: our unit doesn't have this part

info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. Base of robot (see ARI documentation) related artical DFRobot Lidar, RGB-D 3D ToF Sensor, YDLIDAR TG15

items.title: Description
items.text: The ARI robot uses a LIDAR sensor for navigation and mapping. The most common model is the YDLIDAR TG15, as referenced in the Autonomous Navigation Pack and configuration files.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• LIDAR is used for SLAM, obstacle avoidance, and navigation.
  • The YDLIDAR TG15 is the default, but other models may be supported.
  • Vision and laser-based self-localization and mapping (SLAM).
  • Available RViZ and web plugins for navigation.

!speculation: There are mentions of RGBD sensors and other LIDAR models, but YDLIDAR TG15 is most likely.

items.title: LIDAR not detected
items.text:
• Check power and data connections.
  • Verify correct model is set in ROS launch files.

items.title: Poor mapping or navigation
items.text:
• Ensure LIDAR is unobstructed and clean.
  • Check for correct calibration and configuration in ROS.

items.title: Respeaker Microphone Array
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. Head of robot (see ARI documentation) related artical Seeed Respeaker, Seeed Wiki, ODAS, VOSK

items.title: Description
items.text: The Respeaker USB Microphone Array provides far-field voice pickup and is used for speech recognition and sound source localization on the ARI robot.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Plug & Play, compatible with Linux/ROS.
  • Far-field voice pickup up to 5m, 360° pattern.
  • Acoustic algorithms: DOA, AEC, AGC, NS.
  • Publishes filtered audio to /audio/channel0 in ROS.
  • Supports VOSK and Google Cloud backends (VOSK preferred).
  • Can be tuned via ROS for noise suppression.

!speculation: Noise suppression can be adjusted via ROS; may improve performance in crowds.

items.title: Not detected in ROS
items.text:
• Check USB connection and device recognition.
  • Verify ROS node is running and publishing topics.

items.title: Poor audio quality
items.text:
• Adjust noise suppression settings.
  • Test device directly via USB for diagnostics.

items.title: Overview
items.text: This document summarizes the network ports used by the ARI robot and provides troubleshooting tips for common networking issues, including those encountered in virtual machine (VM) or simulator setups.

items.title: Port List
items.text: Port Protocol Usage/Service Notes 22 TCP SSH Remote shell access 53 TCP DNS Domain name resolution 80 TCP HTTP Web UI & REST interface 443 TCP HTTPS Apache server 3000 TCP HTTP Grafana monitoring 8080 TCP HTTP Webcommander 9090 TCP WebSocket ROS communication bridge 11011 TCP HTTP Presentation screen 8000 TCP HTTP-ALT Alternate HTTP 8001 TCP VCOM-Tunnel Knowledge base 8888 TCP Sun Answerbook 2003 TCP Finger 2004 TCP Mailbox 2809 TCP CORBA 6969 TCP ACMSoda

items.title: Example Network Traffic
items.text:
• Port 80: Used for arms control
  • Port 9090: Used for move base

items.title: Duplicate Routes in VM/Simulator
items.text:
If you experience asymmetric connectivity (e.g., robot can ping laptop but not vice versa), check for duplicate routes:

  • Remove conflicting route on eth1: 
    sudo ip route del 192.168.0.0/24 dev eth1
  • Ensure only one active route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network.

items.title: General Tips
items.text:
• Check physical connections and network cables.
  • Verify correct IP addresses and subnet masks.
  • Use ifconfig or ip addr to inspect network interfaces.
  • Use ping and traceroute for connectivity testing.

items.title: modifications
items.text:
to gain more control over the internal networking of the robot system we chose to install our own router in-place of a networking switch that came pre-installed. previously the ari-20c main mother-board acted as the router and dhcp server, with our router now in place we have disabled the dhcp daemon running on ari-20c the following is the steps taken to disable the "dhcp daemon"

pal@ari-20c:~$ su
pal@ari-20c:~$ rw
pal@ari-20c:~$ chroot /ro
pal@ari-20c:~$ systemctl disable isc-dhcp-server.service
pal@ari-20c:~$ systemctl mask isc-dhcp-server.service
pal@ari-20c:~$ exit
pal@ari-20c:~$ ro
pal@ari-20c:~$ exit
pal@ari-20c:~$ sudo reboot

items.title: Overview
items.text: This document explains how to use Docker containers with the ARI robot, including setup steps and troubleshooting common ROS networking issues.

items.title: Common Issue: Container Can't Resolve Hostname
items.text:
This is a common problem with ROS networking in Docker. To fix:

  1. Check if the container can ping itself by hostname: 
    ping $(hostname)
    If this fails, add the hostname to /etc/hosts: 
    echo "127.0.0.1 $(hostname)" | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts
  2. Set the ROS_HOSTNAME environment variable: 
    export ROS_HOSTNAME=localhost
  3. Try running roscore again: 
    roscore &
  4. Verify ROS is working: 
    rostopic list
    You should see at least the /rosout topic.

items.title: Using Explicit IP Addresses
items.text:
If issues persist, configure ROS with explicit IPs:

unset ROS_HOSTNAME
unset ROS_MASTER_URI
export ROS_IP=127.0.0.1
export ROS_MASTER_URI=http://127.0.0.1:11311
roscore &

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
• Ensure Docker containers have network access.
  • Check environment variables for correct ROS networking setup.

items.title: LLM Server
items.text: The LLM Server hosts large language models, providing advanced conversational and reasoning capabilities for real-time autonomous interactions.

items.title: Overview
items.text:
Real Chatbot Implementation: Live Mode enables real-time speech-to-text → LLM → text-to-speech conversations using Ollama models running on the LLM Server.

👉 See also: Live Mode (STT → LLM → TTS) - for real-time autonomous conversation pipeline

Implementation Repository: prgrobots/Rpi-ollama-LLM (Private) - Electron app with Live Mode, model management, and WebSocket STT integration

Note: This is distinct from the PAL RASA intent-based system.

info. Description stock component No contains speculation Yes Location. External server or Jetson/Orin hardware related artical PCPartPicker Example

items.title: Description
items.text: The LLM Server is used to host large language models for ARI, providing advanced conversational and reasoning capabilities. Hardware options vary based on required model size and performance.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Xavier NX: ~GTX 1050 performance, 0.5-1B parameter models.
  • Orin Nano: ~GTX 1650 performance.
  • TX2: GT 1030/MX150, <500M parameter models.
  • RTX 4070 SUPER: 7B-13B parameter models.
  • AGX Orin: 1-2B parameter models.
  • Jetson Nano: <100M parameter models.

!speculation: TX2 is the suggested upgrade for better performance.

items.title: Model runs slowly
items.text:
• Use more powerful hardware (see table above).
  • Optimize model size and quantization.

items.title: Server not detected
items.text:
• Check network connectivity and server status.
  • Verify correct model and dependencies are installed.

items.title: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech
items.text: Live Mode creates an autonomous real-time conversation pipeline where Isabella can listen to speech, process it through an LLM, and respond via text-to-speech.
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Overview
items.text:
Live Mode is an Electron application that manages Ollama LLM models and creates a seamless pipeline for autonomous robot conversations.

Pipeline: 

🎤 Speech Input (WebSocket)
    ↓
📝 STT Server (transcription)
    ↓
🤖 LLM Processing (response generation)
    ↓
🔊 TTS Server (audio output)
    ↓
🎵 Audio Output
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Architecture
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Components
items.text:
• live_mode.js - Core controller managing the STT→LLM→TTS pipeline
  • main.js - Main Electron process with IPC handlers
  • preload.js - Secure bridge exposing APIs to renderer
  • renderer.js - UI event handlers and real-time status updates
  • package.json - Dependencies: Ollama, WebSocket, Electron
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Data Flow
items.text:
1. Speech Recognition - WebSocket connection to STT server captures transcriptions
  2. LLM Processing - Transcribed text sent to Ollama LLM with configured model
  3. Text-to-Speech - LLM response sent to TTS endpoint for audio generation
  4. Input Management - New speech is discarded while LLM processes (prevents interruptions)
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Installation
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Node.js and npm
  • Ollama installed locally or accessible via network
  • STT server running (WebSocket on port 9090)
  • TTS server accessible (HTTP endpoint)
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Setup
items.text:
# Install dependencies
npm install

# Start the app
npm start

# Or run with live mode flag
npm run live
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Usage
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: 1. Install a Model
items.text:
📦 Install Model Section:
├─ Enter model name (e.g., llama2, mistral)
└─ Click "Install" → runs `ollama pull <model>`

Models are downloaded and cached locally. First pull may take several minutes.
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: 2. Start Live Mode
items.text:
🎤 Live Mode Section:
├─ Select a model from dropdown
├─ Verify WebSocket URL (default: ws://192.168.0.100:9090)
└─ Click "Start Live Mode"

The app connects to your STT server and enters listening mode.
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: 3. Interact
items.text: As you speak: - 🎤 appears in Activity Log when speech is detected - 🤖 appears when LLM is generating response - 🔊 appears when sending to TTS - Reply displays the complete response
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: 4. Stop Live Mode
items.text: Click "Stop Live Mode" to disconnect from STT server and halt processing.
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Configuration
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: WebSocket Connection (STT)
items.text:
Default: ws://192.168.0.100:9090

Change in UI before starting, or modify in live_mode.js:

this.wsUrl = 'ws://192.168.0.100:9090';
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: TTS Endpoint
items.text:
Default: http://ari-20c/action/tts

Modify in live_mode.js method _speakText():

const ttsUrl = 'http://your-robot-hostname/action/tts';
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Word Replacements (Pronunciation)
items.text:
Configure pronunciation corrections in live_mode.js:

const replaceList = [
  { joondalup: 'joondalahpppp' },
  { tafe: 'tayfe' }
];
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: LLM Options
items.text:
Current settings in live_mode.js:

const stream = await this.ollamaInstance.chat({
  model: this.modelName,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: text }],
  stream: true,
  format: 'text',
  options: { temperature: 0.3 },
  keep_alive: '5m'
});

Common adjustments: - temperature: 0.1–1.0 (lower = more deterministic, higher = more creative) - keep_alive: How long to keep model in memory after last request - num_predict: Max tokens for response
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Status States
items.text: State Color Meaning started Gray Live mode initialized connected Green WebSocket connected to STT ready Green Waiting for speech input processing Yellow LLM generating response speaking Cyan Sending response to TTS disconnected Gray Connection lost, auto-reconnecting error Red Error occurred stopped Gray Live mode stopped
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Troubleshooting
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: "WebSocket error: ENOTFOUND"
items.text:
Cause: Hostname not resolvable

Solution: Replace hostname with IP address in WebSocket URL field

❌ ws://speech-server:9090
✅ ws://192.168.1.100:9090
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: "WebSocket error: ECONNREFUSED"
items.text:
Cause: STT server not running or unreachable

Solution: Verify server is running and accessible

# Test connection
telnet 192.168.1.100 9090

# Or from Windows PowerShell
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName 192.168.1.100 -Port 9090
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: "Close code 1006"
items.text:
Cause: Abnormal WebSocket closure (network interruption, server crash)

Solution: Live mode auto-reconnects after 3 seconds

  • Check network connectivity
  • Verify STT server is still running
  • Check firewall/proxy settings
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: "TTS error: fetch failed"
items.text:
Cause: TTS endpoint unreachable

Solution: - Verify TTS URL is correct - Confirm TTS server is running - Check network connectivity to TTS host
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: LLM responses are truncated
items.text:
Cause: Max token limit reached

Solution: Increase num_predict in live_mode.js:

options: {
  temperature: 0.3,
  num_predict: 256  // Increase from default
}
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Speech constantly being discarded
items.text:
Cause: LLM processing takes longer than expected (expected behavior)

Solution: Modify _handleMessage() in live_mode.js to queue messages:

// Instead of discarding, add to queue
if (this.isProcessing) {
  this.messageQueue.push(text);
} else {
  this.processMessage(text);
}
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: live-mode-start (IPC)
items.text:
Starts live mode with a specified model and WebSocket URL.

ipcMain.handle('live-mode-start', async (event, modelName, wsUrl) => {
  // Returns: { ok: boolean, error?: string }
});

Parameters: - modelName (string): Name of Ollama model to use - wsUrl (string): WebSocket URL of STT server

Returns: 

{ ok: true }  // Success
{ ok: false, error: "Model not found" }  // Error
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

  // Returns: { ok: boolean, error?: string }

items.title: live-mode-status (IPC)
items.text:
Gets current live mode status.

ipcMain.handle('live-mode-status', async (event) => {

items.title: Adding Conversation Memory
items.text:
By default, each transcription is processed independently. To add conversation history:

// In LiveMode class
this.conversationHistory = [];

// In _processWithLLM()
const messages = [
  ...this.conversationHistory,
  { role: 'user', content: text }
];

// After getting response
this.conversationHistory.push({ role: 'user', content: text });
this.conversationHistory.push({ role: 'assistant', content: response });

// Optional: trim history if too long
if (this.conversationHistory.length > 20) {
  this.conversationHistory = this.conversationHistory.slice(-20);
}
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Custom Prompts
items.text:
Set a system prompt to guide LLM behavior:

const messages = [
  { role: 'system', content: 'You are Isabella, a friendly robot assistant.' },
  { role: 'user', content: text }
];
path: Live Mode: Real-time Speech-to-Text → LLM → Text-to-Speech

items.title: Input Queuing
items.text:
Enable message queuing to process multiple inputs sequentially:

// In _handleMessage()
if (this.isProcessing) {
  this.messageQueue.push(text);
} else {
  await this._processWithLLM(text);
}

// After processing complete
if (this.messageQueue.length > 0) {
  const nextMessage = this.messageQueue.shift();
  await this._processWithLLM(nextMessage);
}

• Small models (3-7B): Fast responses, lower quality
  • Medium models (7-13B): Balanced performance and quality
  • Large models (70B+): High quality, slower responses

For real-time interaction on Raspberry Pi, use small-to-medium models.

Total: 1-8 seconds typical response time

items.title: Limitations & Future Work
items.text:
Current Limitations: - No conversation memory across sessions - Speech discarded during LLM processing (prevents queueing) - No wake word detection - No interrupt capability - Manual TTS URL configuration

Planned Enhancements: - [ ] Persistent conversation memory - [ ] Configurable input queue vs. discard - [ ] Wake word detection ("Hey Isabella") - [ ] Interrupt current response (stop button) - [ ] Streaming TTS (speak while generating) - [ ] System prompt configuration UI - [ ] Multi-turn conversation pruning - [ ] Voice activity detection threshold tuning

the localizer nodes is a wrapper or modification of https://github.com/raulmur/ORB_SLAM2

items.title: tf
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. ?? related artical ros wiki tf understanding ros transforms foxglove

items.title: Description
items.text:
the ari robot system uses the stock ros1 tf system for publishing and solving of all moving parts including its physical location relative to the world and the location of it arms and legs tf uses a one way tree node graph to track the positions and update the positions of components by "one way tree node graph" we mean each component has a parent child relation where a child node will never have a parent node higher up the tree be its child

this tree is encapsulated entirely with in the /tf ros topic so don’t confuse any ros topics with the same name as being the topic witch publishes its location

the components/nodes in the tf tree are called "frames" they represent each components of the robot and other objects in its environment(even non-moving part like a fixed camera ). frames are connected by transformations (position and rotation) relative to another frame as long as there are a chain of transformations between any 2 frames tf will be able to give you back the overall transformation between those 2 frames e.g

if there is a transformation from the world to the base and a transformations from the base to a hand then tf can give you the transformations from the world to the hand this is like a reverse version of how the unity game engine’s game object tree works

!speculation: the top frame in our case is the "/map" or "/world" frame representing its actual real world and ground, it is published by some node relating to the pal_navigation_sm and pal_map_manager ros pagkges

the frame representing the robots relative to the world is either "base_link" or "base_footprint"

the way that way that the transformations between frames are updated are

items.title: Recommended Approach
items.text:
1. Backup - Full system backup before upgrade
  2. Test - Upgrade non-production instance first (if available)
  3. Upgrade - Follow Upgrade Guide
  4. Validate - Run Post-Upgrade Validation
  5. Monitor - Watch diagnostics for 24 hours

items.title: Timeline
items.text:
• Pre-Upgrade Prep: 30-60 minutes
  • Upgrade Process: 45-90 minutes
  • Post-Upgrade Testing: 1-2 hours
  • Total: 2-4 hours

items.title: Official PAL Documentation
items.text:
• ARI 25.01 Manual: https://docs.pal-robotics.com/25.01/ari.html
  • ARI 23.12 Manual: https://docs.pal-robotics.com/sdk/23.12/
  • Upgrade Documentation: https://docs.pal-robotics.com/25.01/management/

items.title: From PAL Robotics
items.text:
• Diagnostics Bug: Higher-level diagnostics may show errors, but lower-level diagnostics will appear green. This is a known PAL issue (see GitHub issue).
  • Node Discovery: First ROS 2 discovery can take up to 1 minute after boot.

items.title: For Isabella
items.text:
• Custom ROS nodes may require recompilation
  • Docker image rebuild required for development environment
  • Network configuration should be re-verified

items.title: Quick Checklist
items.text: Before upgrading, verify: - [ ] Full system backup exists - [ ] All Isabella features documented and tested - [ ] Network configuration documented - [ ] Custom ROS packages backed up - [ ] Team notified of upgrade window - [ ] Post-upgrade testing plan ready

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
1. Review Pre-Upgrade Checklist
  2. Follow Upgrade Guide
  3. Complete Post-Upgrade Validation
  4. Review Component Updates for Isabella specifics

items.title: Support
items.text:
• PAL Support: https://docs.pal-robotics.com/25.01/general/getting-support.html
  • Isabella Docs: See related documentation pages
  • Troubleshooting: Check Post-Upgrade Validation troubleshooting section

items.title: Post-Upgrade Validation & Testing
items.text: Verification steps to confirm ARI 25.01 upgrade was successful and Isabella is fully operational.

items.title: System Access
items.text:
• SSH Access 

    ssh ari@ari-<id>
# Should connect without errors

  • Verify OS Version 

    cat /etc/os-release
# Should show ARI 25.01, Ubuntu 22.04/24.04

  • ROS 2 Verification 

    ros2 --version
# Should show ROS 2 Humble

items.title: Basic Health Check
items.text:
• Network Connectivity 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "ip addr show"
# Should show IP addresses

  • Disk Space 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "df -h"
# Should show adequate free space (> 5GB)

  • Memory 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "free -h"
# Should show available RAM for LLM

  • CPU Temperature 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "sensors"
# Should show normal temperatures

items.title: Dashboard Access
items.text:
1. Open Web Interface
  2. Browser: https://ari-<id>
  3. Login with admin credentials

  4. Should see new ARI 25.01 dashboard

  5. Diagnostics Panel

  6. Click "Diagnostics"
  7. View system health status
  8. Expected: Most items green or stable

  9. Note: Some higher-level items may show yellow (known PAL issue)

  10. System Information

  11. Check listed components

  12. Verify all hardware visible:

    • Cameras
    • LiDAR
    • IMU
    • Batteries
    • Motors/Joints

  13. Device Management

  14. Check connected devices
  15. Verify expected devices present
  16. Note any new or missing devices

items.title: ROS 2 Connectivity
items.text:
# From development machine

# Verify ROS 2 can see robot topics
ros2 topic list

# Should show topics like:
# /amcl_pose
# /scan
# /cmd_vel
# etc.

# Wait 1-2 minutes for full discovery
# Then verify again

items.title: Critical Topics
items.text:
• Camera Topics 

    ros2 topic echo /head_front_camera/color/image_raw
# Should show image data

  • LiDAR Topic 

    ros2 topic echo /scan
# Should show distance data

  • Battery Status 

    ros2 topic echo /power/battery_level
# Should show battery percentage

  • Odometry 

    ros2 topic echo /odom
# Should show robot pose/velocity

items.title: ROS 2 Nodes
items.text:
# Verify key nodes are running
ros2 node list

# Expected nodes should include:
# - Camera nodes
# - LiDAR node
# - Localization node
# - Navigation nodes
# - Etc.

items.title: Live Mode Testing
items.text:
1. Start Live Mode App 

    cd /path/to/live-mode
npm start

  2. Verify Model Selection

  3. Models list populated

  4. At least one model available

  5. Configure Connection

  6. WebSocket URL set correctly
  7. STT server IP configured

  8. TTS endpoint configured

  9. Start Live Mode

  10. Click "Start Live Mode"
  11. Wait for "connected" status

  12. Should show green "Ready" status

  13. Test Full Pipeline

  14. Speak a simple phrase
  15. Observe Activity Log:
    • 🎤 icon appears (speech detected)
    • 🤖 icon appears (LLM processing)
    • 🔊 icon appears (TTS output)
    • Response appears in log

  16. Verify audio output from robot

  17. Stop Live Mode

  18. Click "Stop Live Mode"
  19. Should show "stopped" status

items.title: LLM & Ollama Testing
items.text:
# SSH to robot
ssh ari@ari-<id>

# Verify Ollama running
curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags

# Test model inference
curl -X POST http://localhost:11434/api/generate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model": "llama2", "prompt": "Hello"}'

# Should return streaming response

items.title: Social Perception Testing
items.text:
# Verify person detection
ros2 launch social_perception social_perception.launch.py

# In another terminal, check detections
ros2 topic echo /person_detector/people_detections

# Move person in front of robot
# Should see detection data update

items.title: ROS Nodes Testing
items.text:
# Verify all custom Isabella nodes
ros2 node list | grep -i isabella

# Test each service
ros2 service call /isabella/service_name service_type "data"

# Verify responses

items.title: Camera Testing
items.text:
# View camera stream
ros2 run rqt_image_view rqt_image_view

# Select topic: /head_front_camera/color/image_raw
# Should see video stream from robot's camera
# Verify image quality and clarity

items.title: LiDAR Testing
items.text:
# Launch LiDAR viewer (if available)
ros2 launch pointcloud_to_laserscan pointcloud_to_laserscan_launch.py

# Or check raw data
ros2 topic echo /scan

# Should show distance measurements
# Verify range values are realistic

items.title: Motor Control Testing
items.text:
# Keyboard teleop (if available)
ros2 run teleop_twist_keyboard teleop_twist_keyboard

# Or publish manually
ros2 topic pub /cmd_vel geometry_msgs/Twist "linear: {x: 0.1}"

# Robot should move forward
# Verify motors respond smoothly

items.title: Microphone Array Testing
items.text:
# Test audio input
arecord -l
# Should list audio devices

# Test audio recording
arecord -d 5 test.wav
aplay test.wav

# Should hear recorded audio

items.title: Performance Monitoring
items.text:
1. CPU Usage 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "top -b -n 1"
# Verify not maxed out
# Expected: 30-60% under load

  2. Memory Usage 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "free"
# Verify swap not heavily used
# Expected: < 20% swap usage

  3. Temperature 

    ssh ari@ari-<id> "watch -n 1 sensors"
# Monitor for 5 minutes
# Expected: < 80°C under normal load

items.title: Network Performance
items.text:
# From development machine
ping -c 100 ari-<id>

# Analyze results
# Expected packet loss: 0%
# Expected latency: < 50ms typical

items.title: Long-Running Test
items.text:
• Keep Live Mode running for 10 minutes
  • Monitor for errors or disconnections
  • Check memory usage doesn't creep up
  • Verify consistent response times

items.title: Validation Checklist
items.text: Category Item Status Notes System SSH access ☐ OS version ☐ ROS 2 version ☐ Disk space ☐ Memory available ☐ Web UI Dashboard loads ☐ Diagnostics panel ☐ Hardware visible ☐ ROS 2 Topics available ☐ Camera topic ☐ LiDAR topic ☐ Battery status ☐ Live Mode App starts ☐ Connects to STT ☐ LLM responds ☐ TTS working ☐ Hardware Cameras working ☐ LiDAR working ☐ Motors responding ☐ Microphone active ☐ Stability CPU normal ☐ Memory stable ☐ Temperature normal ☐ Network stable ☐

items.title: "Cannot connect to robot"
items.text:
Solution: 

# Verify network connectivity
ping ari-<id>

# Check if it was renamed during setup
nmap -p 22 192.168.1.0/24

# If found, SSH to IP address
ssh ari@192.168.1.XXX

items.title: "Topics not appearing"
items.text:
Solution: 

# Wait for ROS 2 discovery (can take 1-2 minutes)
# Verify DDS configuration
echo $ROS_DOMAIN_ID

# Check for network issues
ros2 doctor

# May need to reconfigure networking

items.title: "Live Mode won't start"
items.text: Solution: - Verify STT server is running and accessible - Check WebSocket URL configuration - Verify network firewall rules - Check Ollama is running: curl localhost:11434

items.title: "High CPU/Memory Usage"
items.text: Solution: - Verify model size isn't too large for hardware - Check for runaway processes: top - Monitor temperature: sensors - Consider smaller LLM model

items.title: "Audio Issues"
items.text: Solution: - Verify microphone array connected - Check volume levels: alsamixer - Test audio input: arecord test.wav - Verify TTS endpoint accessible

items.title: Post-Validation Steps
items.text:
After confirming all systems operational:

  1. Update Documentation
  2. Note any observed differences from 23.12
  3. Document new features being used

  4. Update Isabella feature list

  5. Team Notification

  6. Announce successful upgrade
  7. Share any new capabilities

  8. Update runbooks/procedures

  9. Continuous Monitoring

  10. Watch system diagnostics for 24-48 hours
  11. Monitor application logs

  12. Report any issues immediately

  13. Backup New System

  14. Create backup of working 25.01 system
  15. Store on external drive
  16. Document backup date/procedures

items.title: Success Criteria
items.text:
Upgrade is successful when:

✅ All systems accessible (SSH, Web UI, ROS 2) ✅ All hardware subsystems operational ✅ Isabella components functioning ✅ Live Mode pipeline working end-to-end ✅ No console errors or warnings ✅ System stable over 24-hour period ✅ Performance meets or exceeds previous version

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Monitor system performance over next week
  • Gradually resume normal operations
  • Report issues to PAL support if discovered
  • Plan future maintenance windows
  • Share lessons learned with team

Estimated Time: 1-2 hours (comprehensive validation) Estimated Time: 15-20 minutes (quick validation) Continue To: Normal operations or troubleshooting as needed

items.title: Pre-Upgrade Checklist
items.text: Complete this checklist before upgrading from ARI OS 23.12 to 25.01.

items.title: Backups
items.text:
• Full System Backup
  • Back up entire robot filesystem
  • Use: sudo tar -czf ari-backup-23.12.tar.gz /home /etc
  • Store on external drive or network location

  • Verify backup integrity: tar -tzf ari-backup-23.12.tar.gz | head

  • ISO Recovery Image

  • Obtain ISO image from PAL Robotics (shipped with purchase)
  • Store on external drive

  • Verify checksum matches PAL documentation

  • Custom Code Backup

  • Back up all custom ROS packages: git clone --mirror <repo> backup/
  • Back up Isabella configurations
  • Back up Docker build scripts

  • Back up any local modifications to robot software

  • Network Configuration Backup

  • Document current WiFi networks
  • Document static IP configurations
  • Document custom DNS settings
  • Document VPN configurations (if used)

  • File: /etc/netplan/ configurations

  • Docker Environment Backup

  • Export current Docker images: docker save -o backup-images.tar <image-names>
  • Back up docker-compose files
  • Back up build scripts and Dockerfiles

items.title: Hardware & Network Verification
items.text:
• Network Connectivity
  • Ping robot: ping ari-<id>
  • SSH connection works: ssh ari@ari-<id>
  • Network latency acceptable (< 50ms typical)

  • WiFi signal strength adequate (RSSI > -70 dBm)

  • Robot Charging

  • Fully charge battery before upgrade
  • Verify charging indicator shows full

  • Robot will be powered down during upgrade

  • Hardware Health Check

  • Access Web UI: https://ari-<id>
  • Open Diagnostics panel
  • Verify all green (or expected states)

  • Note any warnings or errors

  • Subsystems Status

  • Motors respond to commands
  • Cameras stream video
  • LiDAR returns distance data
  • Microphone array functions
  • Speakers output audio

items.title: Software Requirements
items.text:
• Development Environment
  • Docker installed and tested: docker run hello-world
  • Docker compose available: docker-compose --version
  • Sufficient disk space (> 50GB free)

  • Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04 (development machine)

  • PAL Tools

  • Download official upgrade tools from PAL
  • Verify digital signatures if provided
  • Extract to known location

  • Check permissions are correct

  • SSH Keys

  • Verify SSH access to robot works
  • Test: ssh ari@ari-<id> "echo OK"
  • Default credentials working or documented

items.title: Isabella-Specific Checks
items.text:
• Live Mode Verification
  • Live Mode starts successfully: npm start → "Start Live Mode"
  • WebSocket connection working
  • LLM model loads
  • STT server accessible
  • TTS endpoint responsive

  • Document current configuration

  • Custom ROS Nodes

  • All custom nodes compile: colcon build
  • All nodes start without errors
  • All topic subscriptions work
  • All services callable

  • Document node list: ros2 node list

  • Social Perception

  • Person detection working
  • Face recognition enabled/disabled (as configured)
  • Performance metrics noted

  • Document current settings

  • LLM Integration

  • Ollama running and responsive
  • Models loaded and tested
  • Network connectivity to external LLM (if used)

  • Test inference: ollama run llama2 "test prompt"

  • Custom Integrations

  • Any external service integrations working
  • API credentials/tokens verified
  • Network endpoints accessible
  • List all dependencies

items.title: 12 Hours Before Upgrade
items.text:
• Announce Maintenance Window
  • Notify team of upgrade time
  • Document estimated downtime (2-4 hours)

  • Disable any external monitoring/automation

  • Graceful Shutdown

  • Stop all running applications
  • Stop Live Mode and custom services
  • Shut down robot cleanly: sudo poweroff
  • Wait for shutdown to complete
  • Power down robot completely

items.title: Create Recovery USB
items.text:
• Bootable USB Creation
  • Prepare USB drive (8GB+ recommended)
  • Follow PAL instructions to create bootable USB
  • Test USB boot (optional, only if have spare robot)
  • Label USB with "ARI 25.01 Recovery - [date]"
  • Store in safe location

items.title: Pre-Upgrade Records
items.text:
• Capture System State 

    # Log current system information
uname -a > pre-upgrade-system-info.txt
ros2 --version >> pre-upgrade-system-info.txt
docker --version >> pre-upgrade-system-info.txt

  • Document Configuration 

    # Back up ROS configuration
tar -czf pre-upgrade-ros-config.tar.gz ~/.ros ~/.config

# Back up network configuration
tar -czf pre-upgrade-network-config.tar.gz /etc/netplan

  • Create Upgrade Log Template

  • File: upgrade-log-25.01.txt
  • Template sections:
    • Pre-upgrade state
    • Upgrade start time
    • Major steps and timestamps
    • Any errors or issues
    • Upgrade completion time
    • Post-upgrade observations

items.title: Verification Checklist Summary
items.text: Item Status Notes Full system backup ☐ Path: __________ ISO recovery image ☐ Verified: ☐ Custom code backup ☐ Count: __________ Network config backup ☐ Files backed up Docker backup ☐ Images saved Network connectivity ☐ Latency: __________ Robot fully charged ☐ Status verified Hardware diagnostics ☐ All green: ☐ Live Mode working ☐ Tested: __________ ROS nodes verified ☐ Count: __________ Custom services tested ☐ All working: ☐ PAL tools downloaded ☐ Version: __________ Team notified ☐ Downtime OK: ☐ Robot powered down ☐ Time: __________ Recovery USB created ☐ Tested: ☐

items.title: Next Step
items.text:
Once all items are checked, proceed to Upgrade Guide.

Estimated Time: 1-2 hours Point of No Return: When backup USB is created and robot is powered off Last Abort Point: Before booting new ISO image

items.title: Upgrade Guide: ARI 23.12 → 25.01
items.text: Step-by-step instructions for upgrading Isabella from ARI OS 23.12 to 25.01.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text: ✅ Complete Pre-Upgrade Checklist before proceeding.

items.title: Download from PAL Robotics
items.text:
1. Access PAL Download Portal
  2. Contact PAL Robotics support or access your account
  3. Request: ARI 25.01 OS image and upgrade tools

  4. You should receive:

    • ISO disk image (ari-25.01-*.iso)
    • Build docker script (<user-id>-build-docker.sh)
    • Credentials/keys folder

  5. Verify Files 

    # Check file sizes and dates
ls -lh ari-25.01-*.iso
ls -lh *-build-docker.sh

# Verify checksums (if provided by PAL)
sha256sum -c ari-25.01-checksums.txt

  6. Store Safely

  7. Keep original files on external drive
  8. Create working copies for upgrade
  9. Document file paths and checksums

items.title: Requirements
items.text:
• USB drive: 8GB+ (USB 3.0 recommended)
  • Linux machine or tool like Etcher (cross-platform)
  • 15-20 minutes

items.title: Create USB Bootable Image
items.text:
Option A: Using dd (Linux)

# Find USB device
lsblk
# Should show something like /dev/sdb or /dev/sdc

# Unmount if mounted
sudo umount /media/user/USBDRIVE

# Write ISO (CAUTION: specify correct device!)
sudo dd if=ari-25.01-*.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress
sudo sync

# Verify write
sudo eject /dev/sdX

Option B: Using Etcher (GUI, all platforms)

  • Download: https://www.balena.io/etcher/
  • Insert USB drive
  • Select ISO file
  • Select USB device
  • Click Flash
  • Wait for completion

items.title: Verify USB
items.text:
# Re-insert USB and verify files
lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,TYPE
# Should show USB device

# Mount and check
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/usb-check
sudo mount /dev/sdXX /mnt/usb-check
ls -la /mnt/usb-check/
sudo umount /mnt/usb-check

items.title: Robot Setup
items.text:
1. Ensure Robot is Powered Off
  2. Shut down gracefully: sudo poweroff
  3. Wait 30 seconds for complete shutdown

  4. Disconnect power cable

  5. Connect USB to Robot

  6. Insert USB bootable drive into robot's USB port
  7. Use USB 3.0 port if available (faster)

  8. Secure cable to prevent disconnection

  9. Prepare for Boot

  10. Keep monitor and keyboard ready
  11. Or prepare for SSH access after boot
  12. Note: First boot may take 10-15 minutes

items.title: Step 4: Boot from USB and Install
Boot Process
items.text:
1. Power On Robot
  2. Connect power cable
  3. Press power button

  4. Robot should boot from USB

  5. Monitor Boot Sequence

  6. Watch for PAL boot logo
  7. System may show installation prompts

  8. DO NOT interrupt if process begins

  9. Follow On-Screen Prompts

  10. Accept license terms (if shown)
  11. Confirm installation target (should be main drive)
  12. Confirm data will be erased (this is expected)
  13. Allow installation to proceed (typically 30-60 minutes)

items.title: Typical Installation Timeline
items.text: Phase Duration Status BIOS/Boot 1-2 min Checking hardware Installer Load 1-2 min Loading installation files Pre-installation Checks 1-2 min Verifying hardware System Installation 20-40 min Writing files to disk Package Installation 10-30 min Installing software Configuration 5-10 min Setting up system First Boot 5-10 min Starting new OS Total 45-90 min ⏱️

items.title: Monitor Installation
items.text:
Option A: Local Console - Connect monitor via HDMI - Connect keyboard via USB - Watch progress on screen

Option B: Remote SSH (after ~10 minutes) 

# After installation begins, try SSH
ssh ari@ari-<id>
# Should eventually work once system boots

items.title: First Boot After Upgrade
items.text:
The system will boot into new ARI 25.01 OS. You may see:

  1. Welcome Screen - PAL robot setup wizard
  2. Language Selection - Choose your language
  3. Network Setup - Configure WiFi
  4. System Setup - Set hostname, admin PIN, etc.

items.title: Complete Initial Setup
items.text: Follow on-screen prompts to: - [ ] Select language - [ ] Accept license terms - [ ] Configure network (WiFi or Ethernet) - [ ] Set admin password - [ ] Set robot name - [ ] Confirm timezone

items.title: Verify System Booted
items.text:
# SSH into robot (from development machine)
ssh ari@ari-<id>

# Check OS version
cat /etc/os-release
# Should show: Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04 with ARI 25.01

# Check ROS installation
ros2 --version
# Should show: ROS 2 Humble

# Check system is healthy
ros2 topic list
# Should list robot topics

items.title: Safe Removal
items.text:
# From robot console/SSH
sudo eject /dev/sdb  # Or appropriate USB device
# Or physically remove USB

# Shut down gracefully
sudo shutdown -h now

items.title: Power Down
items.text:
• Robot will shut down
  • Wait 30 seconds
  • Disconnect power cable (optional, depends on your setup)

items.title: Step 7: First Boot with Upgraded OS
Power On
items.text:
• Connect power cable
  • Press power button
  • Wait for boot (3-5 minutes typical)
  • First boot may be slower (optimizing)

items.title: Verify Upgrade Successful
items.text:
# SSH to robot
ssh ari@ari-<id>

# Verify OS version
cat /etc/os-release
# Should show ARI 25.01

# Verify ROS 2
ros2 --version
# Should show ROS 2 Humble

# Check basic subsystems
ros2 topic list | head -20

# Access Web Dashboard
# Open browser: https://ari-<id>
# Login with admin credentials

items.title: USB Not Booting
items.text:
Symptom: Robot boots from internal drive instead of USB

Solutions: - Verify USB is bootable: isohybrid ari-25.01-*.iso - Try different USB port (preferably USB 3.0) - Try different USB drive - Check BIOS boot order (may need F2/DEL during startup)

items.title: Installation Hangs
items.text:
Symptom: Installation stuck at certain percentage or step

Solutions: - Wait 10-15 minutes (sometimes just slow) - If still stuck: force shutdown (hold power 10 seconds) - Remove USB, boot from internal drive - Retry from backup recovery using recovery USB

items.title: Network Not Available After Boot
items.text:
Symptom: Cannot SSH or ping robot after upgrade

Solutions: - Check WiFi is configured (see initial setup screen) - Verify network name and password - Try Ethernet cable if available - Use local console to troubleshoot

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
After successful upgrade and first boot:

  1. Proceed to Post-Upgrade Validation
  2. Run diagnostics
  3. Test all Isabella features

  4. Verify functionality

  5. Review Component Updates

  6. Check Isabella customizations

  7. Update any affected code

  8. Monitor System

  9. Watch diagnostics for 24 hours
  10. Monitor temperature and resource usage
  11. Report any issues to PAL support

Estimated Total Time: 2-4 hours Point of No Return: When installation starts Recovery Option: Restore from backup or PAL support
